segments.inferno-18-g35: This whole exclamatory clause opens with the interjection 'Ahi' (expressing pain or pity). 'Come' is an exclamatory 'how'; 'facean' is the archaic imperfect of 'fare' (facean = facevano), 3rd person plural, indicating a habitual or repeated past action ('they would make/they used to make'). 'Lor' (implicit in the line) is an enclitic pronoun meaning 'to them/their' and governs the following infinitive 'levare' ('to raise'); 'le berze' is the direct object (here a body-part noun), with 'le' the feminine plural article, and 'a le prime percosse' is a temporal/conditional phrase meaning 'at the first blows' (preposition 'a' + plural noun phrase). Word order is emphatic/exclamatory, typical of Dante.
segments.inferno-18-g36: 'Già' here is an adverb meaning 'already' or 'by then'. 'Nessuno' is the indefinite subject 'no one' and precedes the verb for emphasis. 'Le seconde' is the feminine plural direct object 'the second ones/blows'; 'aspettava' is the imperfect indicative, 3rd person singular ('was waiting/expected'), describing a continued past attitude. 'Né le terze' adds the negative continuation 'nor the third ones' (né = nor), mirroring the previous object.
segments.inferno-18-g37: 'Mentr'' is a contracted form of 'mentre' meaning 'while'; the apostrophe marks the elision before a vowel. 'Io' is the subject pronoun 'I' (often included for emphasis). 'Andava' is the imperfect indicative of 'andare' ('I was going' / 'I went on'), marking ongoing past action.
segments.inferno-18-g38: 'Li occhi miei' = 'my eyes' with the old masculine plural article 'li'. 'In uno' functions adverbially ('in one [moment]/at once'); 'furo' is an archaic past-tense form (remote past plural of 'essere' — modern 'furono') meaning 'they were'; 'scontrati' is the past participle 'met/collided' agreeing with 'li occhi' and describing the eyes being 'met' or 'encountered'. The construction means the eyes were brought together or met suddenly.
segments.inferno-18-g39: 'E io' ('and I') adds the speaker's immediate reaction. 'Sì tosto' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'so soon' or 'immediately' (sì = so/thus; tosto = soon/quickly). 'Dissi' is the passato remoto 1st person singular of 'dire' ('I said'), recounting a past, completed utterance.
segments.inferno-18-g40: 'Già' again means 'already' or 'by now'. 'Di veder' is the partitive/infinitive phrase 'of seeing' (di + infinitive) used to mark the sphere of acquaintance. 'Costui' is a demonstrative pronoun 'this man/this fellow' (object of 'veder'). 'Non son' is the present indicative of 'essere' with negation ('I am not'); 'digiuno' here is an adjectival predicate meaning 'unacquainted' or 'without experience' (hence the ironic sense 'I'm no stranger to seeing him').
segments.inferno-18-g41: 'Per ch’ io' is a contracted sequence of 'perché'/'per ch'' meaning 'so/therefore' or 'whereupon' plus the subject pronoun 'I' for emphasis. 'A figurarlo' uses the preposition 'a' + infinitive to mark purpose ('in order to make him out/so as to make him out'); 'i piedi affissi' is the object phrase 'my feet fixed/placed' — 'affissi' functions as the verb form (here 1st person past, 'I fixed/placed') or as a participle depending on reading, but the sense is 'I planted/fixed my feet' in order to keep steady and look.
segments.inferno-18-g42: 'E' = 'and'. '’L' is the elided form of 'il' (the). 'Dolce duca' ('sweet/pleasant guide') is an epithet for Virgil. 'Meco' is an archaic enclitic/compound pronoun meaning 'with me' (modern 'con me'). 'Si ristette' is reflexive: 'si' + passato remoto 3rd person singular 'ristette' (from 'ristare' / 'ristare' meaning to stop/stand still), so 'he stopped/paused with me.'
segments.inferno-18-g43: 'E assentio' is 'and (he) assented/consented' — 'assentio' is an archaic past form of 'assentire' ('he agreed'). 'Ch’' introduces the subordinate clause ('that'). 'Alquanto' is an adverb meaning 'somewhat' or 'a little'; 'in dietro' means 'backwards' or 'back'; 'gissi' is an obsolete/poetic form meaning 'I went'/'I stepped' (here used in the subordinate clause with the sense 'that I should step back a little'). The clause as a whole reports that the guide agreed to the speaker's moving back slightly.
segments.inferno-18-g44: E (and) simply links this action to the prior clause. 'Quel frustato' is a past-participial phrase used adjectivally ('that whipped one'). 'Celar si credette' is an inverted sequence: the infinitive celar (to hide) followed by the reflexive clitic si and the passato remoto credette (he thought), so literally 'he thought himself to hide' — i.e. 'he thought himself hidden'. 'Bassando ’l viso' is a gerund (bassando) with the object ’l viso (the face), giving the means by which he hoped to hide (lowering his face).
segments.inferno-18-g45: Ma poco li valse is a short clause: ma (but), poco (adverb: little), li is a dative clitic meaning 'to him' (it does not mean the definite article here), and valse is passato remoto of valere meaning 'it availed' or 'it profited'. The word order places the adverb before the verb for emphasis: 'but it availed him little.'
segments.inferno-18-g46: Ch'io dissi is the connective ch’ (che) introducing the speaker's reported utterance and is followed by the subject pronoun io and the passato remoto dissi (I said). In context the conjunction links the previous clause to what the narrator then spoke.
segments.inferno-18-g47: «O tu che l’occhio a terra gette, is a direct address. O (oh) introduces the vocative; tu is the vocative 'you'; che is a relative pronoun 'who'; l’ occhio (the eye) + a terra (to the ground) is the object/phrase; gette is an archaic/spelling variant of a present-tense verb meaning 'you cast' or 'you throw' (here 'you cast your eyes'). The word order is typical: relative clause after the vocative describes the person addressed.
segments.inferno-18-g48: Se le fazion che porti non son false is a conditional clause. Se ('if') introduces the condition; le fazion ('the features' or 'the looks') is the feminine plural noun; che porti is a relative clause with porti (2nd sing. present) 'you bear/wear' describing those features; non son is the poetic/contracted present of essere ('are'); false agrees in gender/number with fazion. The whole means 'if the features you display are not false.'
segments.inferno-18-g49: Venedico se’ tu Caccianemico is a declarative identification. Venedico is the name vocatively/onomastically given; se’ here is an archaic form representing 'sei' (you are); tu is redundant vocative/emphatic 'you'; Caccianemico is the family name. Word order is emphatic: name + 'you are' + vocative pronoun + surname.
segments.inferno-18-g50: Ma che ti mena a sì pungenti salse? is a direct question. Ma ('but') introduces mild surprise; che functions interrogatively as 'what'; ti is the object/implication 'you' (to you); mena is 3rd-sing. present of menare 'leads/drives'; a is the preposition 'to'; sì (so) + pungenti (adjective 'stinging/pungent') modifies salse (plural noun 'sauces'), used here metaphorically. The clause asks what motive leads him to such biting (figurative) behaviour.
segments.inferno-18-g51: A single period (.) marking the end of the previous sentence and the pause before the reply; here it closes the narrator's speech and the direct question.
segments.inferno-18-g52: Ed elli a me: is a simple reporting clause. Ed (and) links to what came before; elli is an archaic subject pronoun meaning 'he' (form of egli) and here denotes the speaker; a me is a prepositional phrase 'to me', marking the indirect object (he spoke to me).
segments.inferno-18-g53: «Mal volontier lo dico; combines adverbial mal ('badly'/'ill') with volontier ('willingly') to produce the idiom 'mal volontier' = 'reluctantly' or 'unwillingly'. Lo is the neutral direct object pronoun 'it', and dico is 1st-person present 'I say'. The phrase states the speaker's reluctance to speak.
segments.inferno-18-g54: ma sforzami la tua chiara favella, is an imperative/request. Ma (but) contrasts the reluctance; sforzami is a 2nd-person singular imperative of sforzare with enclitic mi ('force/press me' or 'put me to the effort'), here meaning 'press/urge me'; la tua chiara favella is the direct object 'your clear speech' (favella = words/speech).
segments.inferno-18-g55: che mi fa sovvenir del mondo antico is a relative clause modifying 'favella': che = 'which'; mi is the indirect object 'to me'; fa is 3rd-sing. of fare used causatively 'makes' or 'causes'; sovvenir is an infinitive meaning 'come to mind' or 'recall' (so 'makes me recall'); del mondo antico = 'of the ancient world' (i.e., ancient times).
segments.inferno-18-g56: I' (Io) + fui is the passato remoto (simple past) 'I was'; colui che = 'the one who' (colui + relative che). The clause la Ghisolabella condussi places the direct object (la Ghisolabella) before the verb condussi (passato remoto of condurre, 'I led/ brought/caused'); a + infinitive far (archaic contracted form of fare) marks purpose/causation: 'led [her] to make/do the voglia (desire/will) of the marchese.' Word order and tenses are emphatic/poetic but straightforward: past assertion + relative clause explaining what he did.
segments.inferno-18-g57: come che introduces a concessive clause ('although' / 'however'), and suoni is present-subjunctive (suonare) used after the concessive to mean 'however it may sound'. La + adjective sconcia (preceding the noun, poetic word order) modifies novella = 'the indecent/shameful tale/news'.
segments.inferno-18-g58: E 'and'. Non pur (archaic: 'not only') negates/excludes; io is the emphatic subject 'I'; qui is 'here'; piango is present indicative 'I lament/weep'. Bolognese is used predicatively/attributively to indicate origin ('a Bolognese' / 'in a Bolognese manner'). The clause structure emphasizes the speaker's identity while denying exclusivity.
segments.inferno-18-g59: anzi = 'rather/indeed/on the contrary' starts a corrective or amplifying statement. n’ è is an archaic contraction of ne + è; here ne is a locative/partitive element and the construction yields 'indeed it is that this place is so full' (idiomatically, 'this place is so full'). questo loco = 'this place'; tanto pieno = 'so full'.
segments.inferno-18-g60: che introduces a result/explanatory clause: 'that'. tante lingue = 'so many tongues' (i.e. many people/speeches). Non son ora apprese uses son (archaic 'are') + past participle apprese (apprendere) meaning 'have learned' or 'are taught' and ora = 'now/yet' — the sense is 'have not yet learned'. a dicer (archaic infinitive of dire) = 'to say'. tra Sàvena e Reno = 'between the Savena and the Reno' (geographical frame).
segments.inferno-18-g61: e se = 'and if'. di ciò = 'of this/about this'. vuoi is present indicative 'you want' (addressing the listener: 'if you want...'). fede o testimonio = 'faith or testimony' i.e. 'proof or witness' — a formula inviting the hearer to accept the claim or seek evidence.
segments.inferno-18-g62: rècati is the imperative (second person singular) of recare (archaic spelling) meaning 'bring/bring to mind/recall' or 'apply to memory'. a mente = 'to mind' (i.e. 'call to mind'); il nostro avaro seno = 'our greedy/avaricious bosom' (seno used metaphorically for belly/inner part). The phrase is an imperative instruction: 'bring to mind our avaricious bosom' (i.e. remember how greedy we are).
segments.inferno-18-g63: Così parlando is a gerundial phrase 'thus speaking' (simultaneous action). il before percosse is a clitic direct object pronoun ('him/it') placed before the passato remoto percosse (percuotere, 'struck'); un demonio is the postverbal subject (inverted for poetic effect). de la sua scurïada = archaic de + article + possessive + scurïada (scourge/lash), indicating the instrument used to strike.
segments.inferno-18-g64: e = 'and'; disse is passato remoto (he said) introducing direct speech. Simple narrative sequence: action + speech.
segments.inferno-18-g65: Via here is an interjection/exclamation meaning 'Away!' or 'Begone!' rather than its literal lexical senses (road/way); it's used to drive someone off. In speech it functions as a blunt command/exclamation.
segments.inferno-18-g66: Ruffian is a vocative/exclamatory insult — literally 'pander'/'pimp' (one who procures or panders). It names the person addressed and characterizes him contemptuously.
segments.inferno-18-g67: qui = 'here'; non son = 'are not' (son = archaic sono). femmine = 'women' (literal) and da + conio = 'for/from conio' where da + noun indicates function or purpose; conio literally 'coinage/mint' so the phrase is idiomatic and pejorative: 'women to be coined/used' — contextually 'not women to be bought/sold/used' (the demon forbids treating women as merchandise). Word order is standard with negation before verb.
segments.inferno-18-g14: A simple locative prepositional phrase: 'In questo luogo' = 'in this place.' Word order is normal (preposition + demonstrative + noun); the comma sets it off as the scene-setting adverbial.
segments.inferno-18-g15: 'de la schiena scossi di Gerïon' is a participial phrase modifying the travelers: literally 'shaken from the back of Geryon.' 'De' (archaic form of 'di') + 'la schiena' = 'from the back'; 'scossi' here functions as a past participle/adjective (plural) meaning 'shaken' rather than a first‑person verb form; 'di Gerïon' specifies the source.
segments.inferno-18-g16: 'trovammoci' = 'trovammo' (passato remoto, 1st pl.) + enclitic 'ci' (ourselves): 'we found ourselves.' Enclitic attaches to the verb in the older poetic register (trovammoci instead of ci trovammo).
segments.inferno-18-g17: 'e ’l poeta tenne a sinistra' — 'tenne' is passato remoto, 3rd sing. of tenere, here meaning 'kept' or 'held himself' (kept to). The article '’l' = 'il' precedes 'poeta.' 'a sinistra' is the adverbial phrase 'to the left.'
segments.inferno-18-g18: 'e io dietro mi mossi.' — 'mossi' is passato remoto, 1st sing. of muovere, with reflexive clitic 'mi' ('I moved myself' = 'I moved'). 'dietro' is an adverb 'behind/after'; word order places 'dietro' before the clitic + verb for emphasis: 'I moved behind (him/the poet).'
segments.inferno-18-g19: 'A la man destra vidi nova pietà' — 'A la man destra' = 'on the right hand/side' (preposition + article + noun + adjective). 'Vidi' is passato remoto, 1st sing. of vedere ('I saw'). 'Nova pietà' uses the archaic adjective form 'nova' (modern 'nuova').
segments.inferno-18-g20: A simple noun phrase listing what was seen: 'novo tormento e novi frustatori.' 'Novo/novi' are archaic adjective forms (modern 'nuovo/nuovi') agreeing in number/gender with their nouns; 'frustatori' is plural 'scourgers' or those who flog.
segments.inferno-18-g21: 'di che la prima bolgia era repleta' is a relative/explanatory clause: 'di che' = 'of which' / 'of them'; 'la prima bolgia' = 'the first bolgia (ditch)'; 'era' is the imperfect indicative of essere ('was'); 'repleta' is an adjective/past participle meaning 'filled.' Together: 'of which the first bolgia was full.'
segments.inferno-18-g22: 'Nel fondo erano ignudi i peccatori' — 'Nel fondo' (in the bottom/at the depth) sets place; 'erano' is the imperfect of essere ('were'); 'ignudi' (pl.) = 'naked'; 'i peccatori' = 'the sinners.' The imperfect describes the scene/ongoing state.
segments.inferno-18-g23: 'dal mezzo in qua ci venien verso ’l volto' — 'dal mezzo in qua' = 'from the middle on this side' (literally 'from the middle hither'); 'ci' = 'toward us' or 'to us'; 'venien' is an older/poetic 3rd pl. form of venire (they come/they were coming), here best read as present or historical present; 'verso ’l volto' = 'toward the face/our faces.'
segments.inferno-18-g24: 'di là con noi' = 'from over there with us' (di là = from that side/over there; con noi = with us). Short adverbial phrase indicating another stream of movement coming alongside Dante and Virgil.
segments.inferno-18-g25: 'ma con passi maggiori' — 'ma' = 'but'; 'con passi maggiori' = 'with larger/longer/faster steps.' 'Maggiori' is comparative/adjectival plural agreeing with 'passi.'
segments.inferno-18-g26: 'come i Roman per l’essercito molto' — 'come' = 'like/as'; 'i Roman' = 'the Romans' (archaic form 'Roman' for 'romani'); 'per' here has a causal/situational sense 'because of' or 'on account of'; 'l’essercito molto' = 'the great/large host' (word order is slightly inverted but clear in poetic diction).
segments.inferno-18-g27: Temporal apposition: 'l’anno del giubileo' = 'the year of the Jubilee.' It specifies the time/context of the comparison in the previous clause.
segments.inferno-18-g28: 'su per lo ponte hanno a passar la gente modo colto' — 'su per lo ponte' = 'up over the bridge'; 'hanno a passar' is the periphrastic construction 'avere a' + infinitive meaning obligation/necessity or 'must/are obliged to pass'; 'la gente' = 'the people'; 'modo colto' = 'a compact/collected manner' (colto here = 'collected/together' or 'closely packed').
segments.inferno-18-g29: 'che da l’un lato tutti hanno la fronte verso ’l castello e vanno a Santo Pietro' — 'che' introduces the result/consequence clause; 'da l’un lato' = 'from one side'; 'tutti hanno la fronte verso' is idiomatic: 'everyone has their face turned toward'; 'e vanno a Santo Pietro' = 'and go to Saint Peter.' 'Hanno' functions idiomatically ('have' = 'hold/turn' their foreheads that way).
segments.inferno-18-g30: 'da l’altra sponda vanno verso ’l monte' — 'from the other bank they go toward the mountain.' Plain spatial clause: 'da l'altra sponda' (from the other shore/bank); 'vanno' present/plural of andare 'they go'; 'verso il monte' toward the hill/mountain.
segments.inferno-18-g31: 'Di qua' = 'on this side' / 'from here.' Short directional adverbial; 'di' + 'qua' is the idiomatic compound.
segments.inferno-18-g32: 'di là' = 'on that side' / 'from there.' Parallel to the previous phrase, contrasting sides of the rock.
segments.inferno-18-g33: 'su per lo sasso tetro vidi demon cornuti con gran ferze' — 'su per lo sasso tetro' = 'up along the gloomy rock' (su per = up along); 'vidi' passato remoto 1st sing. 'I saw'; 'demon cornuti' = 'horned demons' (adjective postpositive is common in poetry); 'con gran ferze' = 'with great scourges' (gran = grande shortened before a consonant).
segments.inferno-18-g34: 'che li battien crudelmente di retro' — 'che' = 'who/which'; 'li' = object pronoun 'them' (referring to the sinners); 'battien' is an archaic 3rd pl. form of battere/ to beat (they were beating); 'crudelmente' = 'cruelly'; 'di retro' = 'from behind.' The verb + pronoun order (li battien) reflects older/poetic syntax.
segments.inferno-18-g135: E quinci sian le nostre viste sazie»: 'E' is the conjunction 'and' linking to the previous clause. 'Quinci' (from here/hence) indicates point of departure. 'Sian' is the third-person plural present subjunctive of essere used optatively/jussively ('may/let ... be') rather than a plain indicative; modern Italian would normally write 'siano'. The subject 'le nostre viste' (the feminine-plural noun phrase 'our sights/eyes') follows the verb because of poetic inversion, and 'sazie' is a predicate adjective agreeing in gender and number (feminine plural) with 'viste'.
segments.inferno-18-g1: Luogo (noun) is fronted for emphasis, followed by the copula è; in inferno is a locative prepositional phrase (no article, an archaic/poetic usage). Detto is a past participle used adjectivally = "called," linking the name Malebolge to Luogo.
segments.inferno-18-g2: tutto di pietra: tutto (= whole/entire) modifies the following material phrase di pietra = "made entirely of stone." Di color ferrigno is a prepositional phrase meaning "of iron-like colour" (ferrigno adjectival), with the adjective after color in a poetically compact order.
segments.inferno-18-g3: come introduces a simile "like/as." la cerchia is the thing being compared (the enclosing ring); che is a relative pronoun introducing the clause dintorno il volge. Dintorno = "around." Il here is a clitic object referring back to Malebolge ("it"); volge (volgere) = "encircle/turn around" with the ring as subject and the place as object.
segments.inferno-18-g4: Nel dritto mezzo del campo maligno is a locative phrase: Nel = in the; dritto modifies mezzo to mean the exact/straight middle. Vaneggia is used figuratively (intransitive) meaning "gapes/yawns open," and its grammatical subject is un pozzo which follows (poetic inversion). Assai modifies the adjectives largo e profondo (very wide and deep).
segments.inferno-18-g5: di cui = "of which" (relative construction); suo loco = "its place/location" with suo a possessive referring back to the pit; dicerò is future tense "I will tell/describe." l'ordigno = "the structure/design/arrangement" — the direct object of dicerò. Word order places the relative phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.inferno-18-g6: Quel cinghio = 'that belt/girdle' (quel demonstrative + cinghio); che rimane adunque is a relative clause 'which therefore remains.' È tondo is the main predication 'is round.' Tra 'l pozzo e 'l piè de l'alta ripa dura is a locative phrase: between the pit and the foot (base) of the high, hard bank; note contracted articles ('l) and archaic de = di.
segments.inferno-18-g7: e ha distinto = 'and has divided' (ha + past participle distinto = present perfect), in dieci valli = 'into ten valleys/ditches,' il fondo = 'the bottom.' The order places the prepositional phrase before the direct object for rhythmic emphasis.
segments.inferno-18-g8: Quale begins the simile: here it functions like English 'just as'/'such as' and introduces the comparison that follows.
segments.inferno-18-g9: dove per guardia de le mura = 'where, for the guard/defence of the walls' (per indicating purpose); più e più fossi cingon li castelli = 'more and more ditches encircle the castles' (più e più = repeatedly/more and more). Cingon is third-person plural present 'they gird/encircle.'
segments.inferno-18-g10: la parte dove son = 'the part/area where they are (located)' (son = sono). rende figura = 'renders/returns the figure/shape' — rende is 'gives back / reproduces' and figura is the object (the appearance the moats produce).
segments.inferno-18-g11: tale imagine quivi facean quelli = 'there those ones made such images' (facean is the imperfect 'they made' indicating a past, habitual or descriptive action). Quivi is a locative adverb 'there.'
segments.inferno-18-g12: e come a tai fortezze da’ lor sogli a la ripa di fuor son ponticelli = 'and as to such fortresses there are little bridges from their thresholds to the outer bank.' Da' lor sogli is a contracted, archaic way to say 'from their thresholds' (lor = loro/their; sogli = thresholds). Ponticelli is plural 'small bridges.'
segments.inferno-18-g13: così da imo de la roccia scogli movien = 'thus from the very bottom/depth of the rock they set rocks in motion' (imo = lowest/deepest; scogli = rocks; movien = movano/moved — third-person plural imperfect/present-poetic form). che ricidien li argini e ’ fossi = relative clause 'which cut off/cleave the embankments and the ditches' (ricidien = they cut/cleave). infino al pozzo che i tronca e raccogli = 'as far as the pit which severs and gathers them' — che introduces a relative clause where i is the object pronoun 'them' (the embankments/ditches), tronca = 'cuts off/severs', raccogli = 'gathers/collects.' Note archaic word order and contracted forms; che introduces subordinate clauses referring back to antecedents in the previous clause.
segments.inferno-18-g113: Quei is an archaic/demonstrative subject pronoun meaning 'that one' or 'he' (used here as the subject). mi is the clitic object 'me'. sgridò is the passato remoto (third singular of sgridare) meaning 'scolded' or 'upbraided'; the colon marks the beginning of direct speech.
segments.inferno-18-g114: This is the quoted question. Perché functions as an interrogative 'why'. se is the archaic/poetic form of sei ('you are'); tu is the subject pronoun used for emphasis. sì is an adverb 'so' (intensifier). gordo + di + infinitive (gordo di riguardar) is an idiomatic construction meaning 'eager/greedy to look at'; riguardar is the infinitive 'to look at/regard'. più ... che sets up a comparison 'more ... than'; li is the masculine plural article (archaic form), altri brutti = 'the other ugly ones.'
segments.inferno-18-g115: A single period marking the end of the question and the line — punctuation only, no lexical items to analyse.
segments.inferno-18-g116: E io a lui is a short framing phrase introducing the reply: E 'and', io 'I' (subject), a lui is a prepositional phrase 'to him' (indirect object) placed before the quoted speech for emphasis.
segments.inferno-18-g117: Perché at the start of the reply functions as a causal 'because' (not an interrogative); it introduces the explanation that follows.
segments.inferno-18-g118: se ben ricordo here means 'if I remember well'; se is the conjunction 'if' and ben 'well'. ricordo in the text is actually a verb 'I recall' (ricordo), but the provided senses list contains only noun meanings, so the correct verbal sense is not among the listed options.
segments.inferno-18-g119: già = 'already'. t’ is the clitic object for 'ti' (you) attached to the auxiliary ho: t’ho = 'I have you' (i.e., 'I have seen you'). ho is the auxiliary 'have' (present), veduto is the past participle 'seen'. coi is the contraction of con + i = 'with the', capelli 'hair' (plural), asciutti 'dry' (agreeing in number and gender with capelli).
segments.inferno-18-g120: e = 'and'; se’ is the poetic/archaic spelling of sei ('you are') used as the second person singular present of essere; Alessio Interminei is the personal name; da + place = 'from Lucca' (da = from, Lucca = city name). The colon introduces the consequence/explanation that follows.
segments.inferno-18-g121: però here means 'therefore' or 'so' introducing the conclusion; t’ (printed as 'it' in the provided senses) is actually the clitic for 'ti' ('you') and functions as the object of adocchio — the provided senses do not include that reading. adocchio is a first-person present verb meaning 'I eye/notice/single out' (the listed senses are nominal but 'eye' as a verbal sense fits the use). più che introduces the comparative 'more than'; li altri tutti = 'all the other ones' (li is the article, altri 'others', tutti 'all').
segments.inferno-18-g122: Ed is the conjunction e in the form used before a vowel, meaning 'and'. Ellì here is an archaic/poetic subject pronoun equivalent to egli ('he'); the lemma given is misleading, so treat it as 'he' referring back to the person speaking in the context. Allor is an adverb ('then', 'thereupon') giving the temporal frame; the comma marks a short pause before the participial action.
segments.inferno-18-g123: Battendosi is the present participle of battere with the reflexive clitic si attached (battere + si → battendosi), functioning adverbially: 'beating himself' (i.e. beating his head). La is the feminine definite article modifying zucca. Zucca is a colloquial/pejorative word for 'head' here (literal 'pumpkin' but used as slang for skull); together 'battendosi la zucca' = 'beating his head'. The colon introduces the reported speech that follows.
segments.inferno-18-g124: This is direct speech. Qua giù is a doubled adverbial ('here below'/'down here'). M' (m') is the clitic for mi attached before the auxiliary hanno (m'hanno) and functions as the direct-object clitic 'me'; hanno + past participle sommerso forms the compound past (hanno sommerso) meaning 'they have overwhelmed/submerged me'. Le lusinghe is the subject ('the flatteries'). Ond' is a clitic/truncated form of onde/ondè meaning 'from which/whereby' and introduces the result clause; io non ebbi mai is passato remoto 'I never had/experienced'; la lingua stucca uses lingua ('tongue'/'speech') as the thing affected and stucca (metaphorical past/participial use) meaning 'plastered/stopped up'—thus idiomatically 'my tongue was never silenced' or 'I was never kept from speaking.'
segments.inferno-18-g68: I’ is the archaic contraction of io (I). mi is the clitic object pronoun (here reflexive/direct object) meaning 'me' used with raggiunsi. raggiunsi is passato remoto (first person singular) of raggiungere, meaning 'I reached' or 'I joined up with.' con la scorta mia is a prepositional phrase 'with my escort' (la scorta = the escort, mia = my) and completes the verb.
segments.inferno-18-g69: poscia is an adverb 'afterwards/then'; con pochi passi is a measure expression 'with a few steps' (pochi = few, passi = steps). divenimmo is passato remoto first person plural of divenire and here means 'we came/arrived' (not merely 'became'); là ’v’ is a locative phrase 'there where' (’v’ is an archaism for 'ove'/'vo' = where). uno scoglio de la ripa uscia = 'a rock of/from the bank jutted out' — uscia is an archaic form meaning 'jut out / came forth' (from uscire).
segments.inferno-18-g70: Assai is an adverb 'very/quite'; leggeramente is an adverb 'lightly/nimbly.' quel is a demonstrative 'that (one)' referring back to the rock; salimmo is passato remoto first-person plural of salire, 'we climbed' or 'we mounted.' The clause is a simple past statement describing the manner of ascent.
segments.inferno-18-g71: e is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' vòlti is a past participle (turned) used here with an implicit subject (we) to mean 'having turned.' a destra su is a prepositional/adverbial sequence 'to the right, upward.' per introduces the route 'along' or 'up along'; la sua scheggia = 'its splinter/shard' figuratively 'its jagged face' (sua = its; scheggia = sharp projection) describing the rock's surface.
segments.inferno-18-g72: da quelle cerchie etterne ci partimmo is a full clause: da introduces source 'from those eternal circles' (quelle cerchie etterne), ci is the clitic pronoun 'us' (we, used here as the logical object of partire), and partimmo is passato remoto first-person plural of partire 'we departed/left.' The whole means 'we departed from those eternal circles.'
segments.inferno-18-g73: Quando noi fummo is a temporal clause in passato remoto ('When we were'). là dov’ el vaneggia di sotto is a relative locative 'there where it gapes/open beneath' (dov’ = dove = where; el = the/it; vaneggia here = 'gapes open'). per dar passo a li sferzati uses an infinitive construction 'to give passage' (dar passo) followed by an indirect object 'to the sferzati' (li sferzati = the scourged/whipped), indicating purpose or result.
segments.inferno-18-g74: lo duca disse is a simple past report: lo duca (the guide/duke) as subject and disse (passato remoto of dire) 'said.' The article lo before duca is the archaic emphatic or nominative form 'the duke/guide.'
segments.inferno-18-g75: Attienti is an imperative form (older/dialectal) meaning 'hold fast' or 'stand firm/hold on.' It's a direct address from the guide to the addressee and functions as a warning or command.
segments.inferno-18-g76: e fa che feggia is a causative construction: fa (imperative/indicative 'make/see to it') introduces a subordinate clause (che + verb) with feggia (subjunctive/present form of feggiare) 'that [it/them] fashion/turn.' lo viso in te 'the face toward you' places lo viso as the direct object and in te as the direction/target. di quest’ altri mal nati is a genitive/specifying phrase 'of these other ill-born ones.' Grammatically, fa governs the clause que(begins with che) and causes the action described.
segments.inferno-18-g77: ai quali ancor non vedesti la faccia is a relative clause 'to whom you have not yet seen the face' where ai quali = 'to whom,' ancor non = 'not yet,' vedesti is passato remoto second person (here intended 'you saw' but the form is archaic), la faccia = 'the face.' però che son con noi insieme andati is a causal/explanatory clause introduced by però che ('because/for'), with son (they are) + andati (gone) meaning 'because they went with us together.'
segments.inferno-18-g78: Del vecchio ponte guardavam is an imperfect descriptive clause: Del = di + il 'of the' linking to vecchio ponte 'old bridge'; guardavam (imperfect first-person plural) = 'we were watching' or 'we kept watch of.' la traccia = 'the trace/track' (object). che venìa verso noi da l’altra banda is a relative clause 'which was coming toward us from the other side' (venìa is imperfect 'was coming').
segments.inferno-18-g79: e che la ferza similmente scaccia is coordinated to the previous clause: e che = 'and that'; la ferza (the scourge/whip) is the grammatical subject in this clause; similmente = 'in the same way/similarly'; scaccia is present (or historic present) 'drives away' or 'disperses.' The clause reports an effect parallel to the bridge's motion.
segments.inferno-18-g80: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and', linking this clause to what precedes; ’l is the contracted article (il) before a masculine noun; buon is the attributive adjective placed before maestro, giving the customary Dantean collocation 'buon maestro' (my good/masterly teacher). The phrase functions as the noun phrase subject/topic of the reporting clause.
segments.inferno-18-g81: sanza is an archaic form of senza = 'without'; mia is the possessive 'my' modifying dimanda (ask/request). Together 'sanza mia dimanda' means 'without my asking' and is an adverbial phrase modifying the verb that follows.
segments.inferno-18-g82: mi is the indirect-object pronoun 'to me', placed before the verb as normal in Italian; disse is the passato remoto (simple past) of dire: 'he said'. The whole forms the reporting verb 'said to me'.
segments.inferno-18-g83: Guarda is the second-person singular imperative of guardare meaning 'look' (an exhortation addressed to Dante); quel is the demonstrative 'that'; grande is an adjective 'great' describing the person; che is the relative pronoun 'who/which'; vene is an archaic present form of venire (modern viene) meaning 'comes'. Word order is imperative + object phrase + relative clause.
segments.inferno-18-g84: e = 'and' continues the reported speech; per dolor = 'through/for sorrow' (causal: because of sorrow); non par is 'does not seem' (par = pare, archaic present of parere); lagrime is the direct object 'tears'; spanda is 3rd-person present subjunctive (or present indicative in older usage) of spandere 'to pour forth', here used after a perception verb with an implied che: 'it does not seem that he pours forth tears.' The word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.inferno-18-g85: quanto is an exclamatory 'how/ how much'; aspetto here is a noun meaning 'aspect/appearance/ bearing' (this sense is not listed in the provided senses, so it's marked -1); reale modifies aspetto and in context means 'royal' (reale = 'royal'); ancor (ancora) = 'still'; ritene is 3rd-person singular present of ritenere here meaning 'retains/keeps' (literally 'he retains'). The clause exclaims how much of his royal aspect he still keeps.
segments.inferno-18-g86: Quelli is an emphatic or archaic form of quello meaning 'that one' (used as a demonstrative subject); è is the present of essere 'is'; Iasón is the proper name Jason. The clause identifies the person Dante is told to look at.
segments.inferno-18-g87: che introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; per cuore e per senno uses per + noun twice to express means or cause — 'by/through heart (i.e. passion) and by/through sense/wisdom (i.e. cunning)'; li Colchi is the archaic plural article+name 'the Colchians'; del monton = 'of the ram' (del = di + il); privati is a past participle 'deprived' agreeing with 'li Colchi' and féne is the archaic passato remoto form fece + enclitic ne (fece + ne → féne) meaning 'he made them (of it)' i.e. 'he deprived them of it'. The clause thus says he caused the Colchians to be deprived of the ram by means of passion and wit.
segments.inferno-18-g88: Ello is an archaic subject pronoun 'he/it' used in Dante; passò is passato remoto 'passed' (he passed/crossed); per + l'isola di Lenno = 'by/through the island of Lemnos' (Lenno = Lemnos). poi che introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'after' or 'since'; l' ardite femmine spietate = 'the daring/ bold females, merciless' (adjectives after the noun as is common in poetry); tutti li maschi loro = 'all their males' with the possessive placed after the noun for emphasis; a morte dienno = they 'gave to death' i.e. 'put to death' — dienno is an archaic preterite (they delivered/gave), here best rendered 'they put to death.'
segments.inferno-18-g89: Ivi = 'there' (adverb); con segni e con parole ornate is a pair of prepositional phrases 'with signs and with ornate/embellished words' (the repeated con emphasizes the means); Isifile is the subject proper name; ingannò is passato remoto 'deceived' (he/she deceived). The normal object follows (la giovinetta) after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.inferno-18-g90: la giovinetta = 'the young girl' (direct object); che introduces a relative clause 'who'; prima avea is the pluperfect/imperf. auxiliary construction 'had before' (avea + past participle) with prima = 'previously'; tutte l’altre ingannate is the past participle ingannate agreeing with le altre 'the others' (feminine plural): 'who had previously deceived all the others.' Word order places the appositive relative after the noun it modifies.
segments.inferno-18-g91: Lasciolla is a fused/archaic past form: lasciò + la (the past tense 'left' with the enclitic object pronoun 'her'). Quivi is an adverb meaning 'there' (archaic spelling quivi). Together they mean 'he left her there.'
segments.inferno-18-g92: Gravida is a feminine adjective meaning 'pregnant.' It modifies the feminine object 'la' (her) carried from the previous clause; placed after a pause for emphasis.
segments.inferno-18-g93: Soletta is a diminutive/emphatic feminine adjective from sola, meaning 'all alone' or 'left alone.' The -etta/-etta ending intensifies solitude here.
segments.inferno-18-g94: Tal colpa = 'such a fault' (tal = 'such', colpa = 'fault/guilt'). A tal martiro = 'to such a martyrdom/torment' (a marks the goal/recipient). Lui condanna: condanna is present (historic present here) 'condemns'; lui is the disyllabic tonic pronoun 'him' (object) placed before the verb for emphasis — literally, 'to such a torment he condemns him.'
segments.inferno-18-g95: E anche = 'and also.' Di Medea marks the agent/author ('by Medea' or 'of Medea'). Si fa vendetta uses the reflexive/impersonal si plus fare ('fa') to mean 'vengeance is taken' or idiomatically 'Medea takes revenge' (literally, 'revenge is made by Medea').
segments.inferno-18-g96: Con lui = 'with him.' Sen va is the contracted pronominal form se(n) va (se ne va) meaning 'goes away/goes off' (se + ne + va); chi ... inganna is a relative clause 'whoever deceives' or 'those who deceive' (chi = 'who'). Da tal parte = 'from that part' / 'in that way' (lit. 'by that part').
segments.inferno-18-g97: E questo basti: basti is the present subjunctive/jussive of bastare meaning 'let this suffice' or 'this should be enough.' De la prima valle sapere: de (archaic di) + la prima valle + sapere (infinitive) yields an impersonal construction meaning 'to know about the first valley' or 'as for the first valley.' E di color che ’n sé assanna: 'and about those who gnaw/bite at themselves' — che is a relative pronoun; ’n is reduced in-s ('in'); sé 'themselves'; assanna (present) 'gnaws/bites.'
segments.inferno-18-g98: Già eravam: eravam is the archaic imperfect 'we were' (eravamo). Là ’ve = 'there where' (’ve = ove/where). Lo stretto calle = 'the narrow lane/path' (stretto modifies calle). Con l’argine secondo = 'with the second embankment' (argine = embankment; secondo = second). S’incrocicchia is the pronominal verb s'incrocicchiare (si + incrocicchia) meaning 'crosses itself' or 'crisscrosses' (third-person present/historic present).
segments.inferno-18-g99: E fa = 'and (it) makes' (fa = fare, here 'forms' or 'makes'). Di quello = 'of that' (referring back to the embankment). Ad un altr’ arco spalle: literal word order 'to another arch shoulders' — the construction fa di X ad Y spalle means 'makes X the shoulders/support of Y' (i.e., it becomes the shoulders of another arch).
segments.inferno-18-g100: Quindi = 'from there/then.' Sentimmo is the perfect/past 'we heard' (sentimmo). Gente che si nicchia = 'people who cower/whimper' (si nicchia is pronominal, 'makes themselves small/cowers/whimpers'). Ne l’altra bolgia = 'in the other bolgia/ditch' (ne = in). E che col muso scuffa = 'and who with the snout scuff/strike/butt' (col = con + il; muso = snout/face; scuffa = scuffare/scuffa, here 'butts or scuffles with the snout').
segments.inferno-18-g101: E = 'and.' Sé medesma = emphatic reflexive 'themselves' ('sé' = themselves; medesma = very same/emphatic). Con le palme = 'with the palms' (palme = palms of the hands). Picchia is 'strikes/beats' (third-person present/historic present), so 'and they strike themselves with their palms.'
segments.inferno-18-g102: Le ripe: definite article + noun (feminine plural) meaning 'the banks/cliffs'. 'eran' is the archaic/poetic imperfect 3rd-person plural of essere (eraN = erano) = 'were'. 'grommate' is a past participle used predicatively ('were encrusted'); 'd’una' = di + una = 'with/of a' and introduces the material (mould). Word order is regular: article + noun, verb phrase, past participle, prepositional phrase.
segments.inferno-18-g103: 'per' introduces the cause/agent here ('by/through'); 'l’alito' = the breath. 'di giù' = 'from below' (di + adverb), specifying source. 'che' is a relative pronoun ('that') referring back to 'alito'. 'vi' is locative 'there' (i.e. on/at the banks). 's’appasta' is reflexive present indicative of appastare (s' + appasta) meaning 'settles / cakes together / clings' — here: 'that settles there.'
segments.inferno-18-g104: 'che' again introduces a relative clause describing the breath. 'con li occhi e col naso' literally 'with the eyes and with the nose' — instruments by which the breath affects people (sight and smell). 'facea' is the imperfect of fare ('made/caused'), used here in the narrative past to say 'it caused/made'. 'zuffa' is a noun meaning 'a disturbance/scuffle' used figuratively: the breath made a nuisance/assault for eyes and nose.
segments.inferno-18-g105: 'Lo fondo' = the bottom/depth (masculine noun with the definite article); 'è' present indicative 'is'. 'cupo' is an adjective 'dark/gloomy/deep', and 'sì' after the adjective is the intensifier 'so' (so ... that ...). The order article + noun + verb + adjective + intensifier is normal in poetic prose.
segments.inferno-18-g106: 'che' here introduces a result clause ('that'), followed by 'non ci basta' = 'is not enough for us' (ci = 'for us'). 'loco a veder' = 'room/space to see' (infinitive ved­er after a noun expressing purpose). 'sanza' is the archaic form of 'senza' = 'without'; 'montare al dosso' = 'to climb to the ridge/back' (montare + al + dosso). 'de l’arco' = 'of the arch' (de = di, poetic spelling).
segments.inferno-18-g107: 'ove' is an archaic relative adverb meaning 'where'. 'lo scoglio' = 'the rock/crag'; 'più' is the comparative/adverb 'more/most' modifying the verb; 'sovrasta' is present indicative (poetic present) meaning 'overhangs/looms over' — together 'where the rock overhangs most.'
segments.inferno-18-g108: 'Quivi' = 'there' (locative adverb). 'venimmo' is the passato remoto (or poetic past) 1st-person plural of venire = 'we came' / 'we arrived'. The short clause marks arrival at that spot.
segments.inferno-18-g109: This whole clause: 'e quindi giù nel fosso vidi gente attuffata in uno sterco che da li uman privadi parea mosso.' — 'e quindi' = 'and then/from there'; 'giù nel fosso' = 'down into the ditch'; 'vidi' = 'I saw' (perfect); 'gente attuffata' = 'people plunged/immersed' (past participle used adjectivally); 'in uno sterco' = 'in a dung/filth'; the relative clause 'che da li uman privadi parea mosso' = 'which seemed to be moved/stirred by the human privies' (privadi = latrines). Grammatically, 'parea mosso' is 'seemed moved' where 'parea' is imperfect of parere and 'mosso' is a participle describing the dung.
segments.inferno-18-g110: 'E mentre' = 'and while'. 'ch’io' = contraction of 'che io' (subordinating particle + subject 'I') used before a verb; here it introduces the clause 'that I' or simply 'I while...'. 'là giù' = 'down there'. 'con l’occhio' = instrument phrase 'with the eye' (i.e. with my gaze). 'cerco' is present tense of cercare used as a vivid/historical present in narrative meaning 'I scan/look/search'. Word order places the instrument phrase after the locative.
segments.inferno-18-g111: 'vidi' = 'I saw' (past). 'un col capo' = 'one with the head' (col = con + il). The phrase 'sì di merda lordo' is an adjective cluster: 'sì' = 'so', 'di merda' = 'smeared with shit' (di + noun used to indicate material/covering), 'lordo' = 'dirty/filthy'. The whole denotes a person whose head was so besmeared with filth.
segments.inferno-18-g112: 'che' introduces the result/relative clause: 'that...'. 'non parëa' = imperfect 'it did not seem' / 'one could not tell'. 's’era' = contraction of 'si era' (impersonal/reflexive construction) + era (imperfect of essere): here it forms an impersonal phrasing roughly 'one could not tell whether he was ...'. 'laico o cherco' = 'lay or cleric' (laico = layperson, cherco = cleric). In short: it didn't appear whether he was lay or cleric.
segments.inferno-18-g125: Appresso ciò is an adverbial phrase meaning 'after that' (appresso = 'after'); lo duca is the article lo + noun duca ('the guide'/'the leader') in inverted order for poetic style. Fa is the imperative of fare used causatively ('make'/'have'), followed by che introducing a subordinate clause; pinghe is an archaic present subjunctive form of pingere (to paint) governed by the causative 'Fa che' (= 'make him paint').
segments.inferno-18-g126: mi disse is the simple past (passato remoto) of dire with the indirect object pronoun mi 'to me' ('he said to me'). The comma shows the reporting clause interrupting the direct speech.
segments.inferno-18-g127: This continues the direct speech begun earlier. Il viso is the noun phrase 'the face' which in the embedded clause is the object of pinghe ('paint the face'); un poco più avante is an adverbial phrase 'a little farther forward' specifying placement.
segments.inferno-18-g128: Sì che introduces a result/purpose clause ('so that'); la faccia is repeated as the affected thing and ben con l'occhio attinghe uses attinghe (an archaic/poetic subjunctive/indicative form of attingere) meaning 'reach' or 'be reached' with occhio ('eye'/'gaze'). Di quella ... fante is a genitive/qualification 'of that ... harlot', and the relative clause che ... graffia describes her action. Note several archaic/poetic adjectives (sozza, scapigliata) modifying fante.
segments.inferno-18-g129: This gives two alternating present-tense actions of the woman: or s'accoscia ('now she crouches') uses the reflexive particle s' (si) with the verb accosciare; e ora è in piedi stante ('and now she is standing on her feet') uses essere + the locution in piedi + the adjective/participial stante ('standing'). The repetition or/ora is stylistic variation meaning 'now'.
segments.inferno-18-g130: Taïde è is a short nominal identification: Taïde (Thais, proper name) + è, present indicative 'is' — 'It is Thais.'
segments.inferno-18-g131: La puttana is an appositive noun phrase 'the whore' identifying Taïde; che rispuose is a relative clause 'who replied' (rispuose = passato remoto of rispondere). Al drudo suo means 'to her lover' (al = a + il, drudo = paramour, suo = her/his), quando disse introduces the quoted speech; the question Ho io grazie grandi apo te? inverts subject and auxiliary (Ho io = 'have I') and uses archaic apo te for 'from/with you.'
segments.inferno-18-g132: The colon simply marks the lead-in to the direct reported exclamation; there are no lexical items to analyze in this segment.
segments.inferno-18-g133: Anzi is an emphatic/interjective particle here ('on the contrary' / 'indeed'), and maravigliose is a feminine plural adjective 'marvellous' agreeing with the feminine plural noun grazie in the quoted question (the answer modifies 'favors'). The exclamation answers the question just quoted.
segments.inferno-18-g134: A closing full stop; nothing to analyze.

sentences.inferno-18-s9: Ah, how they would make them lift their heels at the very first blows!
sentences.inferno-18-s10: By then nobody waited for the second blows nor the third.
sentences.inferno-18-s11: While I was going, my eyes were suddenly met as one; and I at once said, 'I am not unacquainted with seeing this man.'
sentences.inferno-18-s12: So, to make him out, I planted my feet; and my sweet guide stopped with me, and consented that I should step back a little.
sentences.inferno-18-s13: And that whipped one thought himself hidden by lowering his face; but it availed him little, whereupon I said: "O you who cast your eye to the ground, if the features you wear are not false, you are Venedico Caccianemico."
sentences.inferno-18-s14: But what brings you to such pungent sauces?
sentences.inferno-18-s16: And he to me: "I say it unwillingly; but press me with your clear speech, for it makes me recall the world of old."
sentences.inferno-18-s17: I was the one who led Ghisolabella to do the marquis's will, however indecent the tale may sound.
sentences.inferno-18-s18: And not only do I here lament, a Bolognese; indeed this place is so full that so many tongues have not yet learned to say 'sipa' between the Savena and the Reno; and if you want proof or testimony of this, bring to mind our avaricious bosom.
sentences.inferno-18-s19: Thus speaking, a demon struck him with his scourge and said, 'Away, pander!'
sentences.inferno-18-s20: Here there are no women for the mint (i.e. no women to be used/treated like coin).
sentences.inferno-18-s5: In this place, having been shaken off Geryon's back, we found ourselves; and the poet kept to the left, and I moved behind (him).
sentences.inferno-18-s6: On the right hand I saw new pity, a new torment and new scourgers, of which the first bolgia was filled.
sentences.inferno-18-s7: Down below the sinners were naked; from the middle on this side they come toward our faces, from over there with us but with bigger steps, like the Romans, because of the great host — in the year of the Jubilee — when the people have to pass up over the bridge in a close-packed way; so that from one side everyone has his face toward the castle and goes to Saint Peter, while from the other bank they go toward the mountain.
sentences.inferno-18-s8: On this side, on that side, up along the gloomy rock I saw horned demons with great scourges, who cruelly beat them from behind.
sentences.inferno-18-s45: And from here let our eyes be sated.
sentences.inferno-18-s1: There is a place in Hell called Malebolge, all made of iron-colored stone, like the ring that surrounds it.
sentences.inferno-18-s2: In the exact middle of the malignant plain a pit gapes, very wide and deep; I will describe the structure and location of it.
sentences.inferno-18-s3: That girdle which therefore remains is round between the pit and the foot of the high, hard bank, and it has divided the bottom into ten valleys.
sentences.inferno-18-s4: Just as, where for the defence of the walls more and more moats encircle the castles, the part where they are reproduces the shape, and there those ones made such images; and as to such fortresses there are little bridges from their thresholds to the outer bank, so likewise from the very base of the rock they set rocks in motion that cut through the embankments and ditches down to the pit that severs and gathers them.
sentences.inferno-18-s37: He scolded me: "Why are you so eager to look at me more than at the other ugly ones?"
sentences.inferno-18-s39: And I to him: "Because, if I remember correctly, I've already seen you with your hair dry, and you are Alessio Interminei from Lucca: therefore I take particular notice of you more than all the others."
sentences.inferno-18-s40: And he then, beating his head, said: "Down here they have drowned me in flattery, so that my tongue was never silenced."
sentences.inferno-18-s21: I rejoined my escort; then, after a few steps, we reached the place where a rock jutted out from the bank.
sentences.inferno-18-s22: We mounted it very lightly; and, turning to the right, up along its jagged face, we left those eternal circles.
sentences.inferno-18-s23: When we were there where it gapes open below to give passage to the scourged, the guide said: "Hold fast, and make that the face of these other ill-born ones be turned toward you; to whom you have not yet seen the face, because they went with us together."
sentences.inferno-18-s24: We were watching the trace of the old bridge that was coming toward us from the other side, and that the scourge likewise drives away.
sentences.inferno-18-s25: And my good master, without my asking, said to me: "Look at that great one who comes, and because of sorrow he does not seem to shed tears: how much of his royal bearing he still retains!"
sentences.inferno-18-s26: That one is Jason, who by heart and by wit deprived the Colchians of the ram.
sentences.inferno-18-s27: He passed by the island of Lemnos after the bold, merciless women had put all their males to death.
sentences.inferno-18-s28: There, with signs and with ornate words, Isiphyle deceived the young girl who had earlier deceived all the others.
sentences.inferno-18-s29: He left her there, pregnant and all alone; he condemns him to such a torment for such a fault; and even Medea takes revenge.
sentences.inferno-18-s30: Those who deceive in that way go off with him; and let this suffice concerning the first valley and concerning those who gnaw at themselves.
sentences.inferno-18-s31: We were already where the narrow lane, with its second embankment, crosses itself, and makes of that embankment the shoulders of another arch.
sentences.inferno-18-s32: From there we heard people who cower in the other ditch and who butt with their snouts, and who strike themselves with their palms.
sentences.inferno-18-s33: The banks were crusted with a mould, by the breath from below that settles there, which assaulted both the eyes and the nose.
sentences.inferno-18-s34: The bottom is so gloomy that there is not room for us to see it without climbing to the ridge of the arch, where the rock overhangs most.
sentences.inferno-18-s35: We arrived there; and then down into the ditch I saw people plunged into dung that seemed stirred up by the human privies.
sentences.inferno-18-s36: And while I there below scanned with my eye, I saw one whose head was so fouled with shit that one could not tell whether he was lay or cleric.
sentences.inferno-18-s41: After that the guide said to me, "Make him paint the face a little farther forward, so that the face may be well reached by the eye of that filthy, dishevelled harlot who there scratches herself with filthy nails, and now she crouches and now she stands on her feet."
sentences.inferno-18-s42: It is Thais, the whore who answered her lover when he said, "Have I great favors from you?"
